EPISODE_NARRATIVE: SE Alaska had been under a blocking ridge for three weeks. Finally, a weak front penetrated the ridge on 3/9, but no big deal weatherwise. Next, a slammer of a front moved across the Gulf and made landfall early on the morning of 3/10 and across the Panhandle during the day. First, there were strong winds everywhere. Next, it dumped snow on Hyder. There were thunderstorms on the west side of Baranof Island. No damage reported so far other than a few tree branches down, and big time snow removal at Hyder and Stewart, B.C.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On 3/10 Salmon landing in downtown Ketchikan recorded 73 KT gusts at 1423. The tide gauge on the cruise ship docks recorded 67 KT at 1112. No damage has been reported so far.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two systems moved across the Gulf bringing snow to the northern Panhandle. Cold air in place combined with 2 periods of strong lift in the low and mid levels resulted in a period of moderate to heavy snow early Mon 2/18 and again late Mon afternoon into early Tue morning, 2/19.  Storm totals were highly variable. Impact was slick roads and snow removal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing drizzle was observed around midnight 2/18 with a thin glaze on vehicles in the valley. By morning, 2/18, 1-3 inches new snow had fallen out of the first frontal band. The second, stronger band moved into the area through the afternoon. Storm totals were highly variable ranging from near 8 inches at the Juneau Forecast Office, 7 inches West Juneau, but only 1.5 inches at the airport. Impact was slick roads with some cars in the ditch and snow removal.

Some cold air was trapped over the Yakutat forelands and the northern Panhandle which caused come moderate snow that eventually had some rain mixed. Impact was snow removal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Yakutat ASOS and observer measured a storm total 9.4 inches. Impact was delayed air travel and snow removal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong arctic high pressure built into the Yukon Territory on 2/21 to 2/23 causing Taku winds again for Downtown Juneau and Douglas. One gust was 87 mph at the AJ Dock. Miraculously, no damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were very strong with this Taku wind event on the night of 2/22 and the morning of 2/23. AJ dock had a gust 87 mph at 1704 on 2/22. Numerous gusts were in the 70s. No damage was reported. LLWS was a problem at the Airport, but no accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Deepening storm center tracked into the Eastern Gulf on Sunday evening 4/21 then stalled in the NE Gulf through Mon morning. High winds reached the SW coast of Prince of Wales by midnight then Ketchikan and Edna Bay through early Monday morning. The high winds will continued to move northward through the day as the strong low level jet 70 KT moved north. High pressure building in after the system made the winds persist into Tuesday for the inner channels with a large swell moving onto the outer coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusts to 60 MPH occurred during the afternoon of 4/22. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Modified arctic air had moved over the Panhandle on 1/9 and 1/10 due to offshore flow from colder regions in Canada. Some temperatures had lowered to zero to +10F.  On 1/10 there was a burst of warm moist air that moved over the Panhandle from the south causing numerous winter weather advisories to be issued. Warnings were issued for both the Juneau and Hyder areas due to heavy snow. Impacts: intense snow removal, poor driving conditions, and travel disrupted. The Juneau School District closed schools. ||Details: Bands of snow set up over the central panhandle with the core over Juneau over an extended period of time. Outside of Juneau bands caused heavy snow in Gustavus, Hoonah, Tenakee Springs and Angoon. Prior to the snow reaching the central panhandle, snow fell across the south, changing to rain in Ketchikan and Hydaburg on time, but lasting longer over from Coffman Cove to Edna Bay north. The main low center remained over the far SE gulf/SW of Sitka. This caused NELY flow through inside passages including Klawock/Angoon and down Chatham Strait which kept surface temperatures colder longer, even through temperatures aloft were warming. The prolonged NE flow kept Angoon as below freezing until about noon Friday the 11th, at this time the low was moving inland and weakening. The wind shifted for Angoon enough to change to rain, but remained cold enough for snow to continue in the Juneau area through 4pm Friday. However, during the day Friday the flakes became more icy or pellets, which caused the light fluffy snow that had fallen all day Thursday to compact down significantly and limit additional depth - altough observers in Angoon and Hoonah noting still needing to clear a lot of snow off their vehicles multiple times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow for the Juneau area, 1/10 to 1/12 was 11 to 17 inches. Outlying areas may have gotten up to 20 inches in this storm. Impact was intense snow removal. Driving conditions were impassable in some instance. Public schools were closed in Juneau on 1/11.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm force low at 988 MB in the central Gulf of Alaska moved NW to Middleton Island on the night of 10/10. Strong surface pressure differences occurred for most of the Panhandle on 10/11.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Yakutat Airport ASOS measured gusts as high as 51 mph. Estimated wind in the area was 60 mph based on known windier areas around town. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On New Year's Day 2019, a strong push of warm air moved over SE Alaska. Most areas had windy conditions and temperatures above freezing. This warm air had plenty of moisture and daily some rainfall records were set. The remained cold at elevations along the Klondike Highway for heavy snow, where the impact was poor driving conditions and snow removal. The other problem was storm force winds. The Ketchikan area got hit with winds above 60 mph, but no damage was reported. Due to the heavy rain AND wind, Thorne Bay had a significant debris flow that closed the highway to and from the town. No injuries or damage was reported from that incident, but DOT spent considerable effort to reopen the road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Salmon Landing near Downtown Ketchikan observed numerous measured gusts 60 to 65 mph during the day on 1/1.  No damage was reported. The wind also impacted the slide at Thorne Bay. Please see: 	||https://www.krbd.org/2019/01/02/big-landslide-blocks-road-into-thorne-bay/	||which has pictures.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow visible in Klondike web cams starting around 01/0430. The Klondike Hwy was closed as of 12/31 1538. DOT report Road closed due to severe weather on Canadian side, only flurries at the time but blowing snow. We figure that the highway on the US side got a dump of snow only to have it compacted and melted by the time the highway opened and temperatures warmed dramatically. By 1100, DOT reported that there was only 3 inches of snow on the road at the Pass. Impact was snow removal and closure of the highway on a holiday.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 1037 MB High pressure over the Yukon on 1/9 moved over northern Canada on the evening of 1/9. This system was opposed to a  983 MB low center SW of Dixon Entrance on the afternoon of 1/10. Surface pressure differences caused very strong winds on the night of 1/9. No damage was reported, but the Alaska Marine Highway system canceled sailings in Lynn Canal due to storm force wind and heavy freezing spray.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of sustained wind 40 to 50 mph with gusts up to 86 mph occurred after midnight for Downtown Juneau and Douglas on 1/9 through the evening of 1/9. No significant damage was reported. Impact was that actions were taken by customers to mitigate Taku winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 1037 MB High pressure over the Yukon on 1/9 moved over northern Canada on the evening of 1/9. No damage was reported, but the Alaska Marine Highway system canceled sailings in Lynn Canal due to storm force wind and heavy freezing spray.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of sustained wind 40 to 50 mph with gusts up to 85 mph occurred on the evening of 1/9 for Skagway. No significant damage was reported. Impact was that actions were taken by customers to mitigate these extreme North to NE winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 968 mb low moved rapidly northward through the western Bering Sea, bringing cold air over the western Aleutians, which intensified the already strong winds. Storm force winds moved across the Bering, bringing high winds to the Pribilof Islands, and blizzard conditions to the Kuskokwim Delta region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Shemya ASOS reported winds gusting to 78 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 968 mb low moved rapidly northward through the western Bering Sea, bringing cold air over the western Aleutians, which intensified the already strong winds. Storm force winds moved across the Bering, bringing high winds to the Pribilof Islands, and blizzard conditions to the Kuskokwim Delta region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: St Paul airport ASOS gusted to 75 mph during this event. The city cancelled events at school and church due to the wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 968 mb low moved rapidly northward through the western Bering Sea, bringing cold air over the western Aleutians, which intensified the already strong winds. Storm force winds moved across the Bering, bringing high winds to the Pribilof Islands, and blizzard conditions to the Kuskokwim Delta region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mekoryuk ASOS reported visibilities less than 1/4 mile for over 4 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 968 mb low moved rapidly northward through the western Bering Sea, bringing cold air over the western Aleutians, which intensified the already strong winds. Storm force winds moved across the Bering, bringing high winds to the Pribilof Islands, and blizzard conditions to the Kuskokwim Delta region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kipnuk experience three hours of blizzard conditions during this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 955 mb low pressure system moved into Bristol Bay, bringing an occluded front through the southern portion of the state. As the front approached, winds began to gust through channeled areas, including Turnagain Arm and the Anchorage Hillside. This was a classic winter wind event for Anchorage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several mesonet stations recorded winds gusts above 73 mph between noon and 4 p.m. The highest gust recorded was 81 mph off Potter Valley Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 955 mb low pressure system moved into Bristol Bay, bringing an occluded front through the southern portion of the state. As the front approached, winds began to gust through channeled areas, including Turnagain Arm and the Anchorage Hillside. This was a classic winter wind event for Anchorage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portage ASOS recorded wind gusts above 73 mph for two hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An occluded front associated with a strong low in the Gulf of Alaska moved through the state, bringing snow and wind to the Kuskokwim Delta. This caused near blizzard conditions for several hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Chevak reported visibility of less than 1 mile in snow and winds gusting to 35 mph during this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly developing low pressure system crossed the western Aleutian Islands and moved northward into the Bering Sea. The associated frontal system brought hurricane force winds to the Central Aleutians.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Adak recorded wind gusts up to 89 mph at the airport ASOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm force low pressure system in the western Gulf of Alaska weakened to 972 MB by the afternoon of 3/13. A strong associated front moved over the Panhandle on the evening of 3/13 with a low level jet 60 to 70 mph out of the southeast. Surface winds were observed up to 65 mph for Prince of Wales and the Ketchikan area during that evening although no damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ketchikan Tide Gauge observed gusts to 67 mph during the evening. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm weather in the western Susitna caused snow and glacial melt in the upper reaches of the basin. A prolonged period of warm weather in late June and early July caused melting of high altitude snow and glaciers in the Alaska Range. At Talkeetna, the nearest weather station, daily highs in this time period were all above normal, with records being broken or tied on 6/23, 6/28, 7/5, 7/6, 7/7 and 7/8. With regards to winter snowpack, the NRCS April 2019 Snowpack Report states that upper elevation snow in the Alaska Range appears to be above normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Above normal temperatures combined with above normal high elevation snowpack caused this flooding event.  A local lodge reported 6-7 inches of water in one of their guest cabins.  The Yentna River at Lake Creek (YLKA2) crested at 21.82 feet on 7/1/2019.  Moderate flood stage is 22 ft, minor flood stage is 20.5 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure centered over the eastern Beaufort Sea combined with low pressure stretched across the Arctic Coastal plain resulted in persistent easterly winds gusting 40 to 60 mph along the eastern Arctic coast. The combination of strong winds and falling snow created areas of blowing snow and blizzard conditions at times for two days. Winds diminished and conditions improved when the pressure gradient weakened.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong east winds gusting as high as 58 mph combined with falling snow resulted in blizzard conditions with visibility below 1/4 of a mile at times. The strongest winds and lowest visibility were reported at the Pt. Thompson (PAAD) oil field.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 978 millibar low pressure system moved into the northeast Gulf of Alaska and continued north into the Yukon Territory. The associated weather front spread precipitation from the Alaska-Yukon Border west to the Fairbanks area. In many locations the precipitation began as rain or a rain snow mix and eventually transitioned to all snow. The weather front remained nearly stationary for 36 hours before dissipating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heaviest snow fell at higher elevations across the Tanana Yukon Uplands. The SNOTEL site at Eagle Summit on the Steese Hwy at an elevation of 3650 feet reported an estimated total of 11 inches.  Less snow was observed at lower elevations because precipitation began as rain before transitioning to snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 985 millibar low pressure system moving into the Western Bering Sea combined with strong high pressure over the mainland Alaska resulted in strong southerly winds through the Bering Strait.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tight pressure gradient across the Bering Strait and northern Bering Sea resulted in southerly wind gusts from 40 to over 60 mph over the marine area and along coastal capes. The strongest winds were reported at Wales (PAIW) where gusts to 61 mph were observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved northward from the Gulf of Alaska into Southcentral, deepening to 985 mb as it crossed the Cook Inlet. This cause high winds through Turnagain Arm and across the Upper Hillside.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: McHugh Creek RWIS site gusted to a max of 71 mph during this time.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusts to 60 MPH occurred during the afternoon of 4/22 in Haines Harbor. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest flow aloft brought relatively warm moist air over the Panhandle on the afternoon of 2/5. Cold air was trapped in many places with favorable conditions for first for freezing rain, then snow. On the night of 2/5, a cold front stalled along the outer coast for additional accumulation of new snowfall. Snow persisted, although light, into 2/7 and was truly a dump. ||Freezing drizzle was observed at the beginning of the event, changing to snow by 2000. The Yakutat sounding showed a deep dry layer in the snow growth region. This region was saturated through the night with a change to snow. Juneau WFO began observing  snow at  2200 on 2/5 with a glaze beforehand.  The front then followed and precipitation ended from north to south.  Although some areas including Ketchikan stayed snow far longer than originally forecast, likely due to light winds, heavier rates, and better forcing aloft.  ||The big impact was heavy snow removal. Storm totals ranged from 6 to 12 inches new snowfall from this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall 4 to 5 inches plus freezing rain. The impacts were  slippery conditions and snow removal.

On the night of 2/5, a cold front stalled along the outer coast for additional accumulation of new snowfall. Snow persisted, although light, into 2/7 and was truly a dump. ||Freezing drizzle was observed at the beginning of the event, changing to snow by 2000. The Yakutat sounding showed a deep dry layer in the snow growth region. This region was saturated through the night with a change to snow. Juneau WFO began observing  snow at  2200 on 2/5 with a glaze beforehand.  The front then followed and precipitation ended from north to south.  Although some areas including Ketchikan stayed snow far longer than originally forecast, likely due to light winds, heavier rates, and better forcing aloft.  ||The big impact was heavy snow removal. Storm totals ranged from 6 to 12 inches new snowfall from this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall 8 to 10 inches plus ice fog, glaze, and freezing rain. The impacts were  slippery conditions and snow removal. The snow amounts varied widely in surrounding areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak thermal trough over the mainland of Alaska allowed thunderstorms to develop in the Matanuska Valley. Steering flow was very light, making the storms almost stationary. Combined with poor drainage, this produced localized flooding in Palmer, Alaska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Palmer ASOS reported 0.44 of precipitation between 5:32 p.m. and 6:00 p.m. This caused localized flooding of parking lots in the Palmer area. Quarter inch hail was also reported at this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Some very strong thunderstorms developed over the southern end of Southeast Alaska on the evening of June 27th due to cold air aloft moving over unseasonably warm surface temperatures. 300 lightning strikes occurred in 15 minutes along with gusty outflow winds. No damage was reported in that the cells were over a wilderness area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning detection 300 strikes in 15 minutes. No wind observation available. Wilderness area. No damage reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm weather in the western Susitna caused snow and glacial melt in the upper reaches of the basin.  A prolonged period of warm weather in late June and early July caused melting of high altitude snow and glaciers in the Alaska Range.  At Talkeetna, the nearest weather station, daily highs in this time period were all above normal, with records being broken or tied on 6/23, 6/28, 7/5, 7/6, 7/7 and 7/8.  With regards to winter snowpack, the NRCS April 2019 Snowpack Report states that upper elevation snow in the Alaska Range appears to be above normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Above normal temperatures combined with above normal high elevation snowpack caused this flooding event.  The Yentna River at Lake Creek (YLKA2) crested at 21.82 feet on 7/1/2019.  Moderate flood stage is 22 ft, minor flood stage is 20.5 ft.  Many lodges were surrounded by water and one reported 6-7 inches of water in one of their guest cabins.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Snow Glacier Dammed Lake (GDL) on the Kenai Peninsula releases, on average, every other year. The 2019 release was an unusually high amount of water. This release was also earlier than past years, which normally occur later in the water year when water levels are low.  The ensuing flood caused erosion and damage along the railroad tracks downstream of the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Aerial monitoring of the Snow Glacier Dammed Lake, combined with a rise in the water level on the river gauge indicated the lake had released. The forecast river stage was based on the estimated amount of water in the lake. Primrose Campground and Cooper Lake Campground were flooded, closing the campgrounds. The Alaska Railroad experienced high levels of erosion along the route due to shifting of the river channel, in addition to the high volume of water. The railroad had to close the route for a week during peak tourist season.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving north along the west coast of Alaska produced significant snow across the western Interior.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across the western Interior with 6 to 10 inches of new snow, heaviest along the Nulato Hills. The NWS automated snow depth sensor at Kaltag reported an estimated 8 inches of new snow during the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system in the Bering Sea pushed a warm front across southern Alaska. The storm brought moisture from much further south, contributing to heavy rainfall in Kodiak. In Southcentral, high winds were experiences as the front crossed the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: McHugh Creek RWIS station observed a peak gust of 84 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system in the Bering Sea pushed a warm front across southern Alaska. The storm brought moisture from much further south, contributing to heavy rainfall in Kodiak. In Southcentral, high winds were experiences as the front crossed the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall caused erosion and mudslides along the Chiniak Highway. Some parts of the road were washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly developing low pressure system moved from the North Pacific into Bristol Bay. The central pressure decreased from 973mb to 963mb as it crossed the Alaska Peninsula. The attached front crossed the Kenai Peninsula and brought warning level winds to Turnagain Arm and the Anchorage Hillside.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portage ASOS measured a peak wind of 81 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 974 MB low in the central Gulf had a series of strong, associated fronts move onshore over the Panhandle on 9/20. This caused a burst of wind that caused some some tree blowdowns and some minor damage in Juneau.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although no wind observations in the Juneau area on 9/20 measured 60 MPH, there were several blowdowns of trees and power outages. One vehicle was damaged by a tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusual wind event hit Haines on the afternoon of 8/17. A low pressure system at 1003 MB moved into Dixon Entrance but there was a 1042 MB high over northern Alaska. A strong northerly blew at Haines. Eldred Rock got to 60 mph, but the Downtown Haines observation from the Marine Exchange was only 30 mph. Minor damage was reported at Haines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Chilkat Daily news reported wind damage during the afternoon of 8/17. Eldred Rock got to 60 MPH gusts. Boats were beached and small trees were uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the night of October 5th through the morning of the 6th, two weather systems merged over the Northeast Gulf of Alaska producing a strong and moist weather front. This front moved through Southeast Alaska causing strong winds and heavy rain. Complex (mountainous) terrain and strong winds as the front moved over the Juneau area resulted in enhanced rainfall in the early morning hours of October 6th. ||Localized flooding on small creeks and debris flows occurred around the downtown Juneau area, Douglas and along Thane Road during the overnight hours. John Street, on Douglas Island, saw massive erosion when culverts were overwhelmed or blocked by debris from the rainfall runoff. This event produced highly variable 24 hour precipitation totals ranging from just over one inch at the Juneau airport to more than seven inches in Douglas with most rainfall falling in 6 to 12 hours. This was a very anomalous event with return periods ranging from a 200-500 year event in Douglas to a 10-25 year event in downtown Juneau for 12 and 24 hours time frame.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most of the damage from the erosion when culverts were overwhelmed or blocked and debris flows that blocked roadways in Douglas were reported by trained weather spotters or emergency management right after midnight on October 6th. There was multiple calls from the public located on St. Anns Ave about minor street flooding in Douglas along with a debris flow on David and John Street washing out portions of the street. Police started to evacuate people out of harms way with the Red Cross helping people displaced from the damage. Just before sunrise more reports came in that Thane Road was blocked by a debris flow just before Mt.Roberts street. There was also other reports in the morning of the 6th that there was a debris flows in Cope Park in downtown Juneau along with major damage to the Alaska Electric Light and Power's flume system as a debris took out a section of the flume. One report came in that a person's oil tank became dislodged from erosion and was spilling heating fuel into the ground and street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest flow aloft will enhance snowfall along the Klondike Hwy while cold are remains in place. Snow accumulation at the higher elevations were expected in the 6 to 8 inch range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow spread in Sunday night to White Pass and by 7 am Monday morning snow had tapered off and started to mix with rain.  A wet 3.5 inches was measured at the top of the pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Higher than average winter snow pack (180% of average) in the Koyukuk watershed resulted in high water for several villages along the Koyukuk River. The village of Allakaket experienced the most significant impacts. There were two distinct peaks in water levels due to fluctuating warm temperatures well above freezing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In the village of Allakaket, several roads were inundated including First and Second Avenue. Water also flowed over the road adjacent to the store. Several outbuildings were flooded and/or moved to higher ground. The local observer estimates water levels would need to rise 2 feet to flood the lowest elevation house. There were no known permanent structures impacted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong synoptic front with 70 kt low level jet at 850 mb. Over-running caused snow at White Pass and in Hyder.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hyder observed a measured amount 9.6 inches of snow fell overnight. Snow had stopped as of observation time, but started again later. An additional 6 inches of snow were reported on the morning of the 27th and a power outage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overrunning snow event for interior areas of Misty Fjords with freezing air anchored in interior valleys near US - British Columbia Border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moist overrunning the cold air in the southern panhandle. The lower levels warmed overnight and early the 17th the snow changed to rain.  8.8 inches was reported as falling on the morning of the 17th, Snow depth at site rose 10 inches in depth.  Snow from overnight had changed over to rain before climate day.

The visibility was reduced to one quarter mile or less in snow and blowing snow. There was a peak wind gust of 52 kt (60 mph) at the Gambell AWOS.
Strong front moved onshore with good moisture transport and cold enough temperatures at the border stations  for snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Skagway DOT had 9 inches of snow and blowing snow at White Pass in their morning report.  Skagway Customs estimated 7 inches of snow at their station and passed on a report of 3 ft drifts at the Pass.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Haines DOT estimated 6 inches of snow on the Haines Highway at 440 am on the 28th.  Haines Customs COOP station measured 8 inches of snow on the 28th and still snowing lightly.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm moist air aloft was lifted over cold air at the surface over the northern Lynn Canal area on the morning through the evening of 12/5. This resulted in significant, new snowfall ranging 6 to 13 inches over 12 hours. The impacts were snow removal for the towns and highway system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Klondike Highway got the most snow, but amounts were highly variable from White Pass to Skagway. New snow amounts ranged from 5.4 inches at Dyea to an estimated 10 inches on the Klondike Highway. Impacts were snow removal and poor driving conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An overrunning snow event for the Chilkat Valley including Haines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over running snow event starts early on the 18th and continues to early on the 19th.  Snow amounts at daybreak in the Haines area was 7 to 8 inches.  By late afternoon roughly a foot of snow had fallen. Final 2 day totals in the Haines area were 12 to 15.5 inches.

A southerly jet stream brought several low pressure systems to Southcentral Alaska. These were accompanied by above freezing temperatures, abundant rainfall, and high winds as the fronts passed through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Primary impact of this episode was flooding of the Anchor Point River.  North Fork Road at mile 15.5 was impassable, water was over the Sterling Highway in several locations between mile 161 and 163, flooding across East End Road at Bear Creek Drive and a mudslide on East End Road at Kachemak Bay Drive.  Water also flooded the Anchor River State Recreation area and surrounded multiple recreation cabins in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed south of the Aleutian Chain and then moved northward along the Alaska Peninsula and up Cook Inlet. A strong high pressure system behind it brought a large amount of cold air behind the low. This created the perfect scenario for snow and high winds along the Alaska Peninsula and northward through the Cook Inlet area. Snow and blizzard conditions continued into January 2020.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Toksook Bay ASOS reported over 24 hours of blizzard conditions. Blizzard conditions continued into January 2020.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system in Prince William Sound combined with a high pressure system over the Interior to increase winds through channeled areas. The winds combined with snowfall and transportable snow on the ground created blizzard conditions in Thompson Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thompson Pass DOT recorded 10 of snow in 24 hours. They experienced northeasterly winds at 40 mph with visibility near zero at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stretch of cold weather resulted in a freeze up ice jam on Willow Creek in the MatSu Borough.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Late on December 21, an ice jam on Willow Creek near the Deneki Bridge (61.77N, -149.956W) caused water to back up and flow through numerous residences.  Deneki Road and access to the Deneki Bridge was closed for multiple weeks between December 21 and January 8, 2020.  At least thirteen households evacuated on December 21-22 due to flooding or anticipation of road closures.  Six homes were damaged by flood water; there was a report of knee-deep water in one home, as well as deep flowing water around the house, ���It formed a river in my yard��� .  Numerous, (>10), cars were flooded and frozen in place.  Also some cars had to be abandoned after owners tried to drive across the flood waters and became stuck.      ||Examining the hydrographs, the most likely cause of the ice jam flooding was an ice dam which collapsed in the canyon, causing a mini breakup which was constricted and stopped at the Deneki Bridge.  ||The MatSu Borough worked with private contractors to divert the water and allow access to the Deneki Bridge and the subdivision of homes and farms to which it provides the single point of access.  Using heavy equipment, they mechanically removed the ice jam, which allowed the water flow to return to the channel, preventing it from flowing through the neighborhoods overland.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pilot Point ASOS reported over 24 hours of blizzard conditions. Blizzard conditions continued into January.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy snowfall rates were observed on the Hyder webcam overnight and through the morning of the 28th. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 965 mb low pressure system in the Gulf of Alaska brought warm air aloft over Southcentral. Meanwhile, a 1025 mb high pressure system over the Arctic Ocean held cold air in place at the surface. The large pressure difference between these two system also caused windy conditions around Southcentral. Precipitation in areas that had warm air aloft fell as rain, while areas that remained below freezing in the upper levels received snow. This contributed to blizzard conditions in some areas as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Alaska Department of Transportation confirmed 6 to 10 inches of new snow through Thompson Pass. RWIS site at Thompson Pass indicated winds gusting to 35 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved over Bristol Bay and brought a front through Southcentral Alaska. The funneling of the winds through mountainous terrain caused high winds throughout Anchorage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet observation in Bear Valley recorded a gust to 73 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On November 27th, a complex and rapidly developing low pressure system moved from the North Pacific towards the Kenai Peninsula. The initial set-up showed a low pressure system in Bristol Bay, extending a warm front through the southern Kenai Peninsula. This low pressure center continued to intensify as it pushed the warm front through the southern portion of Kachemak Bay.  The combination of the rapid drop in pressure with the southeastern approach of the warm front dramatically increased southeasterly winds across Southcentral. The front also brought warm air overrunning cold air in the Susitna Valley, leading to a record snow event for the western side of the Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eaglesong Lodge reported 30 inches of snow in 24 hours, with a storm total of 46 inches. This was the greatest single event total in 26 years of records.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved northward from the Gulf of Alaska towards Bristol Bay. This brought a large amount of warm air into the region. Preceding conditions across Southwest Alaska were well below freezing. The warm air aloft allowed the precipitation in the area to fall as rain, which froze instantly upon hitting the ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bethel ASOS recorded 0.25 inches of precipitation at 10 a.m. This had all fallen as freezing rain. The Kuskokwim School District called around 5:55am to report sleet and freezing rain; said they already had 0.2 of ice accumulation. School was canceled across the Kuskokwim District. Bethel PD reported no power lines or tree branches down, 5 reports of vehicles sliding off road but no injuries.  The doors on the National Weather Service Bethel autosonde froze and could not open for 12z launch on 11/6.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 998 mb low moving quickly from west to east across the Beaufort Sea resulted in strong westerly winds as the associated weather front swept across the Arctic Coast of Alaska. Wind gusts in excess of 60 mph were observed on the eastern Arctic Coast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough of low pressure with a long fetch from the North Pacific to the Gulf of Alaska produced a very strong and wet weather system. The atmospheric river slammed into northern southeast Alaska panhandle from September 20th through the 21st. The northern inner channels received moderate to heavy rainfall with stations reporting one to two inches of at sea level with more at higher elevations. Along with the heavy rainfall there was very high freezing levels which ranged from eight to ten thousand feet. These high freezing level accompanied with the heavy rain increased the runoff from any snow left in the mountains and ice melt from area glaciers. All of the rain and snow/ice melt produce moderate flooding along the Taiya River and flooded the Chilkoot trail, part of the Klondike Gold Rush National Historical Park, near Dyea Alaska. The river crested around seventeen point sixty-four feet which is above moderate flood stage of seventeen feet in morning hours of September 21st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a rain event to prime soils a day before the flooding event after a very dry period for the northern inner channels of Southeast Alaska. Moderate to heavy rains moved over the area on September 20th. The high rain rates continued for the next forty-eight hours. Rainfall totals from this time frame ranged from one to two inches near sea level with higher amounts in the headwaters at higher elevations. The very warm temperatures created very high freezing levels that increase runoff from snow or ice melt. The Taiya River started to rise through the afternoon of September 20th and went above minor flood stage of sixteen point five feet by the early evening hours. The river continued to rise at steep fashion through the night and went above moderate flood stage of seventeen feet a little past midnight of September 21st. The Taiya River crested in the early morning of September 21st at seventeen point sixty-four feet. The rain slowly diminished through the rest of the morning hours and the river slowly receded to be below minor flood stage by the afternoon of September 21st. The National Park Service reported that there was some flooding Chilkoot trail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure center tracked north through the Bering Sea across St. Lawrence Island, the Bering Strait, and into the Chukchi Sea between Aug 2-4th, 2019. This was the strongest in a series of weather systems associated with an atmospheric river which developed over the Bering Sea from late July and into August. This early fall storm resulted in coastal flooding, high surf, erosion, and riverine flooding due to heavy rainfall. Impacts included minor flooding of homes, seasonal subsistence camps damaged, road washouts, and coastal lagoons flooded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm surge ranged from 6 to 10 feet above the normal high tide line in eastern Norton Sound. The high surge combined with southwest winds 30 to 45 mph resulted in high surf and significant wave runup. In Shaktoolik, water surrounded the village, much of the normally dry tundra was inundated, but erosion of the evacuation route out of town was the biggest impact. In Unalakleet, water was up to the AC store, the Fish processing plant, around the village and the airport was surrounded by water. In Stebbins, minor flooding was observed and a few homes were surrounded by water. Lastly, in St. Michael minor flooding of the old shipyard and erosion were the biggest impacts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure pressure system over the eastern Arctic moved south into the eastern North Slope and Brooks Range. Unseasonably cold air with this system produced significant snowfall at high elevations along the North Side of the Brooks Range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather front dropping south from the Arctic resulted in significant upslope snowfall along the north side of the Brooks Range from the Dalton Hwy eastward.  The Imnaviat Creek NRCS SNOTEL at approximately 3000 feet in the foothills of the Brooks Range recorded an estimated 6 inches of snowfall on Aug 20-21st. The nearby UAF Toolik Field Research Station also reported accumulating snow during this event. It is likely higher amounts fell but were unreported.

The high surge combined with southwest winds 30 to 40 mph resulted in high surf and significant wave runup. In Kotlik, Flooding reached some homes and structures and water got into the floor insulation in homes in low lying areas. The warnings helped people to prepare and move property, such as snow machines, four wheelers, and fish nets, to higher ground or indoors.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of weather fronts associated with an an atmospheric river moved into the Bering Sea and across Interior Alaska during the first week of August. Rainfall totals between Aug 1-8th ranged from 5 to 10 inches across the Alaska Range. This resulted in mudslides and flooding that impacted Denali National Park and the community of Healy.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 10 inches of rain fell in Denali National Park the first 8 days of Aug 2019. Saturated soils in steep terrain resulted in multiple mudslides that impacted the Denali Park Road. A larger slide on Aug 4th closed the road at MP48 for several hours. Due to the excessive rainfall and rock/mudslides, the NPS took preventative measures and closed the road beyond MP30 Teklanika Rest Area until conditions improved.||Saturated soils combined with over 4 inches of rain the first week of August in Healy caused Dry Creek to rise overbank on Aug 6-7th and resulted in flooding.  The flooding washed out several roads, driveways, and the approach to the Tipple Bridge which is used to access infrastructure at the Usibelli Coal Mine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of weather fronts associated with an an atmospheric river moved into the Bering Sea and across Interior Alaska during the first week of August. Heavy rainfall resulted in flooding of the Dalton Highway near the Jim River from Milepost 142 to 145.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system move east through the Beaufort Sea brought blizzard conditions and strong winds to the eastern Arctic Coast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure centered over the eastern Beaufort Sea combined with low pressure stretched across the Arctic Coastal plain resulted in persistent easterly winds gusting 40 to 60 mph along the eastern Arctic coast. The combination of strong winds and falling snow created areas of blowing snow and blizzard conditions at times for two days. Winds diminished and conditions improved when the pressure gradient weakened.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong east winds with frequent gusts over 40 mph resulted in blizzard conditions with visibility less than 1/4 of a mile at times. The lowest conditions were reported at the village of Nuiqsut (PAQT).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 978 millibar low pressure system moved into the northeast Gulf of Alaska and continued north into the Yukon Territory. The associated weather front spread precipitation from the Alaska-Yukon Border west to the Fairbanks area. In many locations the precipitation began as rain or a rain snow mix and eventually transitioned to all snow. The weather front remained nearly stationary for 36 hours before dissipating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heaviest snow was reported at higher elevations in the hills north and east of Fairbanks. A resident at Milepost 46 of the Steese Hwy northeast of Fairbanks reported an estimated 24 hour snowfall of 8.0 inches. Less snow was observed at lower elevations because precipitation began as rain before transitioning to snow. In the Fairbanks area, 2 to 4 inches of snow was observed.

In many locations the precipitation began as rain or a rain snow mix and eventually transitioned to all snow. The weather front remained nearly stationary for 36 hours before dissipating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heaviest snow fell at higher elevations across the Fortymile Country. The SNOTEL site at Jack Wade Junction on the Taylor Hwy at an elevation of 3585 feet reported a 24 hour snowfall of 8.4 inches. The COOP observer in Chicken reported 6.5 inches. Less snow was observed at lower elevations because precipitation began as rain before transitioning to snow.
